figures.caption: Fig. 4–1. The electric field E\FigE at point (1)(1), from a charge distribution, is obtained from an integral over the distribution. Point (1)(1) could also be inside the distribution.
Fig. 4–2. The work done in carrying a charge from aa to bb is the negative of the integral of F⋅ds\FigF\cdot d\Figs along the path taken.
Fig. 4–3. In carrying a test charge from aa to bb the same work is done along either path.
Fig. 4–4. The work done in going along any path from [math]a to [math]b is the negative of the work from some point [math]P_0 to [math]a plus the work from [math]P_0 to [math]b.
Fig. 4–5. The flux of [math]\FigE out of the surface [math]S is zero.
Fig. 4–6. The flux of [math]\FigE out of the surface [math]S is zero.

Fig. 4–7. Any volume can be thought of as completely made up of infinitesimal truncated cones. The flux of [math]\FigE from one end of each conical segment is equal and opposite to the flux from the other end. The total flux from the surface [math]S is therefore zero.
Fig. 4–8. If a charge is inside a surface, the flux out is not zero.
Fig. 4–9. The flux through [math]S is the same as the flux through [math]S'.
Fig. 4–10. The flux through a spherical surface containing a point charge [math]q is [math]q/\epsO.
Fig. 4–11. Using Gauss’ law to find the field of a uniform sphere of charge.
Fig. 4–12. Field lines and equipotential surfaces for a positive point charge.
Fig. 4–13. Field lines and equipotentials for two equal and opposite point charges.
